summary: It's been wild ITP with TSA agents calling out sick at Hartsfield-Jackson creating travel chaos while ICE agents patrol the terminals. Meanwhile, Reynoldstown finally sees construction moving on that cleared corner near the BeltLine, and Decatur's getting some nice missing middle housing options.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown BeltLine Corner Finally Breaks Ground After Five Years
topStory.summary: That cleared corner at Chester and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action after sitting empty since 2021. Embry Development is taking over from Toll Brothers to build 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. Construction crews have started groundwork on what will become Camber Crossing, mixing townhomes and condos in one of Reynoldstown's prime BeltLine-adjacent spots.
topStory.body:
If you've walked the BeltLine lately, you've probably wondered what was happening with that big empty lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace. Good news: after nearly five years of nothing, construction is finally moving forward on what will become Camber Crossing.

Embry Development Company picked up this 2-acre site from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago, inheriting permits for 85 residential units about a block west of the Eastside Trail. The original buildings were cleared back in 2021, leaving neighbors wondering if anything would ever actually get built there. Now groundwork is underway for a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos in one of Reynoldstown's most coveted BeltLine-adjacent locations.

This kind of infill development is exactly what the neighborhood has been waiting for as property values climb and more people want to live within walking distance of the trail. The project sits right in that sweet spot where Memorial Drive meets the BeltLine corridor, putting future residents steps away from some of the city's best bike and pedestrian infrastructure.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown

Embry Development is taking over from Toll Brothers to build 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. Construction crews have started groundwork on what will become Camber Crossing, mixing townhomes and condos in one of Reynoldstown's prime BeltLine-adjacent spots.
stories.body:

Embry Development Company picked up this 2-acre site from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago, inheriting permits for 85 residential units about a block west of the Eastside Trail. The original buildings were cleared back in 2021, leaving neighbors wondering if anything would ever actually get built there. Now groundwork is underway for a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos in one of Reynoldstown's most coveted BeltLine-adjacent locations.

This kind of infill development is exactly what the neighborhood has been waiting for as property values climb and more people want to live within walking distance of the trail. stories.imageAlt: Construction site at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Missing Middle Housing Village Opens Near Decatur
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is launching next week with 18 for-sale houses built around shared courtyards on Midway Road. It's designed as more accessible ITP housing near downtown Decatur, where median home values have climbed past $700,000. The pocket neighborhood is turning two underused lots into village-style housing with a focus on community-oriented design.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices hitting stratospheric levels, a new project is trying to offer something more attainable without forcing people to move OTP. Cottages at Midway is having its soft launch next week, featuring 18 standalone houses arranged around shared green space on Midway Road in unincorporated Decatur.

The village-style development sits on just 2.1 acres that used to be two underused lots, turning them into what the developers call "missing middle" housing. That's the sweet spot between single-family McMansions and big apartment complexes. With downtown Decatur's median home values now over $700,000, projects like this could help keep the area accessible to people who aren't tech executives or hedge fund managers.

What makes this interesting is the intentional community design. Instead of just cramming in as many units as possible, the houses are arranged around shared courtyards and greenspace. It's the kind of setup where you might actually know your neighbors' names, which feels pretty radical in today's Atlanta housing market.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway development

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to the West End near the BeltLine. Ackerman & Co. started construction last year and the first seven units are nearly topped out. It's a rare opportunity to buy new construction townhomes right along the Southwest BeltLine trail.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the Southwest BeltLine lately, you've probably noticed construction moving along at 600 Hopkins Street. That's GW Parkside, formerly known as Hopkins and White Townhomes, and it's starting to look like actual buildings.

Ackerman & Co. (the same folks behind Lee + White) started demolition a year ago and now the first seven of 23 planned townhomes are nearing their rooflines. This is one of the few chances to buy brand-new townhomes right along the BeltLine in Southwest Atlanta, with the first units hitting the market this spring according to the signage on site.

For-sale townhomes are pretty rare along the BeltLine these days, especially in the West End where most new construction has been apartments or condos. The location puts you within walking distance of the trail and all the Lee + White food and retail action, which should make these pretty popular once they're finished.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The menu features soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa, Peking chicken platters, and cocktails like Thai tea martinis. Both chefs bring serious credentials from working with Gordon Ramsay and other high-end kitchens.
stories.body:
Inman Park just got a serious new addition with Mushi Ni opening today on Elizabeth Street. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez have finally made the jump from pop-ups to their own brick-and-mortar spot, and the timing couldn't be better with spring weather bringing people back to the Freedom Park Trail.

The menu hits all the comfort food notes with an Asian-American twist. We're talking soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa, crab Rangoon puffs, and their signature Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. The cocktail program looks equally thoughtful, with drinks like tamarind whiskey sours and Thai tea martinis that should pair perfectly with the umami-heavy food.

Both chefs bring legitimate experience to the table. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, while Le has been making waves in the local scene for years. Their location right off the trail should make it a natural stop for anyone walking or biking through the neighborhood.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Texas-Style Smokehouse Opens at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: John Lewis brought his Austin barbecue expertise to the BeltLine with a new location behind The Cook's Warehouse. The smokehouse upstairs features six 22-foot smokers running 24/7, turning out some of the best brisket in Atlanta. Lewis helped weld the smokers himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, creating what looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia.
stories.body:
The new Lewis Barbecue at Ansley Mall has the most impressive smokehouse setup I've ever seen in Atlanta. Instead of hiding the smokers in some back alley, they put them upstairs in a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six massive 22-foot smokers fill the space, running 24 hours a day in staggered eight-hour shifts.

Pitmaster John Lewis, who made his name in Austin and Charleston, helped weld these smokers himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks. The factory rhythm they've established turns out some legitimately great brisket, along with the kind of loaded platters that balance smoke and heat perfectly. The location behind The Cook's Warehouse puts you right in the heart of the Ansley Mall development.

What sets this apart from other barbecue spots around town is the serious commitment to the craft. This isn't fast-casual barbecue or some corporate chain. Lewis knows what he's doing, and the setup here gives him the tools to do it right. Plus, being able to see the smokehouse operation through those big windows adds a theater element that makes the whole experience more engaging.

stories.headline: ICE Agents Patrol Hartsfield-Jackson as TSA Chaos Continues
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are now working at the world's busiest airport to help with security as the government shutdown enters its sixth week. TSA agents are calling out sick at record rates, creating four-hour wait times and massive lines. Mayor Dickens assured residents the ICE agents won't do immigration enforcement during this deployment.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson has gotten genuinely surreal, with armed ICE agents in tactical gear patrolling the terminals while travelers wait in security lines for hours. The partial government shutdown has TSA agents working without pay for over a month now, and they're calling out sick at rates we've never seen before. On Sunday, 41% of TSA agents in Atlanta didn't show up for work.

Mayor Dickens announced the ICE deployment on Sunday, emphasizing that the agents are only there for general security support, not immigration enforcement. That's probably cold comfort for anyone who's spent their morning in a line that snakes through multiple terminals. The airport is telling people to show up four hours early, which is absolutely wild for domestic flights.

This whole situation highlights just how fragile our transportation infrastructure really is. When federal workers can't afford to keep showing up because they're not getting paid, the ripple effects hit everyone trying to travel through what's supposed to be the world's most efficient airport. The longer this shutdown drags on, the worse it's going to get.

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Project Nearly Finished
stories.summary: The West Howard Avenue transformation from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle is almost done after nearly a decade of planning. The project includes a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures. It's designed to make the corridor more people-friendly instead of just a drag strip for cars rushing between Decatur and Atlanta.
stories.body:
After years of watching cars fly down West Howard Avenue like it's their personal speedway, Decatur's Complete Streets project is finally wrapping up. The roughly one-mile stretch from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High) to Paden Circle (by East Lake MARTA) has been transformed from a suburban drag strip into something that actually works for people on foot and bikes.

The centerpiece is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers running most of the length, giving cyclists a protected space separate from both cars and pedestrians. New sidewalks, infrastructure upgrades, and traffic calming measures round out the package. This has been in the works for nearly a decade, so seeing it actually take shape feels pretty significant.

What's particularly smart about this project is how it connects different parts of the transportation network. You've got the East Lake MARTA station on one end and downtown Decatur on the other, with the new bike infrastructure making it much easier to get between them without a car. It's the kind of infrastructure investment that should pay dividends for years as more people look for alternatives to driving everywhere.
stories.imageAlt: New cycle track and sidewalks on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls as Fire Union Fights Mayor
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in city limits and requiring APD to document ICE activities. Dozens of residents also criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract that council ratified last year. The full council votes on both ICE measures April 20.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is taking a strong stance on federal immigration enforcement with two resolutions that passed committee unanimously Monday. One would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits, while the other requires Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. Both measures head to the full council for a vote on April 20.

But the bigger fireworks came from dozens of firefighters and residents who showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighter union contract. Council ratified the deal in April 2025, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since, leaving the firefighters in limbo. The timing is particularly awkward given the ICE agents now patrolling the airport.

This is exactly the kind of local-versus-federal tension we're seeing play out across the country. City council is trying to limit federal immigration enforcement within Atlanta, while the mayor is dealing with the practical reality of needing federal help at the airport because of the shutdown. Meanwhile, the firefighters are caught in the middle of a political standoff that has nothing to do with their ability to do their jobs.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with firefighters in attendance

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb County
stories.summary: A seven-year-old boy died overnight after accidentally shooting himself at a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood. Family members told reporters the child was playing with a firearm when it discharged. DeKalb County police are treating it as an unintended weapon discharge and investigating the circumstances.
stories.body:
A tragic accident in DeKalb County claimed the life of a seven-year-old boy who accidentally shot himself while playing with a gun overnight. The incident happened at a home on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area, drawing a large crowd of grieving family and friends to the neighborhood.

Family members at the scene told reporters the boy was playing with the firearm when it went off. Police investigators confirmed off-camera they're treating this as a "non-intended discharge of a weapon" while they work to determine exactly what happened. The roadway was blocked for several hours during the initial investigation.

This kind of preventable tragedy highlights the ongoing issue of unsecured firearms in homes with children. Every year in Georgia, kids find guns that weren't properly stored and accidents like this happen. It's a stark reminder that gun safety isn't just about training adults, it's about making sure weapons are completely inaccessible to children who don't understand the consequences of their actions.
stories.imageAlt: Police investigation scene in DeKalb County

stories.headline: Two Separate Shootings Rock Atlanta Tuesday Night
stories.summary: A man was killed outside a food mart in Vine City on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard, while another shooting on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive left one person wounded and a child injured by debris. Atlanta police are investigating both incidents but haven't connected them. The MLK shooting shattered windows at a barber shop.
stories.body:
Tuesday night was particularly violent in Atlanta with two separate shootings less than a mile apart. The deadlier incident happened just before 9 p.m. at a food mart on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard in Vine City, where a man was shot and later died at the hospital. Police haven't released his identity or any information about suspects.

The second shooting occurred about 30 minutes later on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive, where someone opened fire outside a barber shop. One person was shot and drove themselves to the hospital in stable condition, while a child was struck by debris and suffered non-life-threatening injuries. The barber shop's windows were completely shot out, with glass scattered across the sidewalk.

While both shootings happened in the same general area within a short time frame, Atlanta police haven't indicated they're connected. The violence underscores ongoing safety concerns in these neighborhoods, where residents and business owners are trying to build community while dealing with the reality of gun violence that can erupt without warning.
stories.imageAlt: Police scene at Joseph E. Boone Boulevard shooting

stories.headline: 8-Foot Python Shows Up on Atlanta BeltLine
stories.summary: Someone captured video of a man handling a massive snake found along the BeltLine, but wildlife officials are warning people not to touch unfamiliar animals. The python isn't native to Georgia and it's unclear whether the person in the video was the owner or just a good Samaritan. Southeastern Reptile Rescue called the situation concerning.
stories.body:
The Atlanta BeltLine has seen its share of unusual sights, but an 8-foot python definitely ranks near the top of the weird list. Video captured someone pulling the massive snake out of bushes along the trail, though it's not clear if the person handling it was the owner or just someone who decided to help out.

Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue told reporters the video is concerning because pythons aren't native to Georgia and can be dangerous if handled improperly. The snake appears to be a Burmese python, which can grow much larger and poses risks both to the person handling it and to local wildlife if it gets loose in the ecosystem.

This is exactly the kind of situation where good intentions can go really wrong really fast. If you see an 8-foot snake on the BeltLine, maybe don't try to wrestle it yourself. Call the professionals and let them handle it safely. Plus, if someone is keeping exotic pets like this, there are legal requirements they need to follow to make sure animals don't end up loose on public trails.
